speaker: interviewer
text: Anytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So the mail came to your house.

speaker: interviewee
text: The mail came to my house. My mother went and bought a-- an Easter egg from paper-- which you could put together, put some candy in there-- put the letter on the bottom, put the candy on top, gave me the Easter egg, said, get on the bus, ride out to the barracks, and say-- see that you deliver it to what they call Werkmeister Rosenzweig. Well, I, I got there. And there was a guard in front of the barracks. And a little guy-- little kid comes in and says-- I was 10 years old about at the time-- I have a Easter egg for Werkmeister Rosenzweig. He said, go ahead, go ahead, and pointed which building it was. And I brought him the Easter egg. And that was my first involvement in conspiracy against Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHTER] He did-- it-- he did get to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: He get-- he subsequently quit the police in, in Beuthen. And he got to Palestine. And I know-- and my aunt who had met him-- my aunt who lives in, in Israel, as a matter of fact-- I saw from some letters I found lately that she had heard of him, that he had gotten to Israel, and was in the police. Or some-- not-- yeah, she was-- he was in the British police school where he was being trained together with Arabs to be in the British police in Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: There comes a point when your family has to leave Beuthen.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1933, in spring. And again, this is to me anecdotal, the, the-- some of the details. Because at the time-- 11 years old or 12, shortly before my 12th birthday-- I didn't understand all the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --goings on. My father was called to the Gestapo. Apparently, they had not liked some of the things he had written in the paper. And they told my father that he has to get out of Beuthen. Now, I had to explain that when my father joined the paper, which was in Poland, we were, by the fact that Katowice had become Polish in 1922, Polish citizens, a fact which-- of which, I, in 1932, at first was not aware either. When the paper hired my father, they made as a conditions that he kept the Polish citizenship. Even so, until 1928, he had been entitled to apply to receive the German citizenship. But the paper, which was owned by Germans, didn't want it because they were afraid the Poles could kick him out if he is a German citizen. They would not allow it-- give him a working permit.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now we are Polish citizens. My father is called to the Gestapo. And they tell him, get out from here. My father immediately packed some stuff because he was afraid they were going to arrest him.

speaker: interviewee
text: But before that happened, my still-- parents were called to the school and were told that I wouldn't be allowed to finish the school year-- which in Germany at that time ended in April-- and that the next year, I would not be allowed to enroll anymore. And even so, that principal of the school was a personal friend of my parents. He gave them to understand that that was not his doing. He could not say so outright. Apparently, he was afraid already that somebody might be listening in or whatever. But that was the situation as it was. And so my father immediately rented a room with a family-- furnished room and stayed in Poland. And the whole family went on a vacation into Poland temporarily for the summer because we couldn't get an apartment in Katowice. There was shortage. But the biggest problem was I didn't speak a word of Polish at the time. And I had to go to school in Poland. I couldn't go to a Polish school because, first of all, my father still worked for a German newspaper. But besides, I also didn't speak Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I enrolled into a private German high school, where Polish was one of the obligatory subjects, but where after I had passed an entrance examination in all the other subjects, I had been exempted from a Polish examination under the condition that within the first school year, I would make up and be able to-- from the second year on in that school to follow the Polish classes as everybody else did, which I did. So I spent the summer, first of all, in a place near Bielitz-- Bielsko. Bielitz it was in German. That was former Austria, and during-- until the end of World War I. It's spelled B-I-E-L-I-T-Z. In Polish, Bielsko, B-I-E-L-S-K-O.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went in the suburbs there, which was called Gypsy Forest. And I spent most of the days-- my teacher was a, a high school student in his last year, just before he went to the university. And he murdered me with, with Polish classes from morning to late afternoon. I had barely time to go a little bit on the swimming pool while I was there. And then in fall, I entered the school there. And in January of 1934-- by January 1934, we had an apartment found in Katowice, And the whole family moved to Katowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody was living in Katowice then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was in Katowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you and your father separate from your mother and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father-- we separated from my mother and sister on-- about two weeks, two weeks, three weeks before the outbreak of the Second War.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Second War, War broke out-- the, the war broke out in the morning hours of September 1, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, before we get--

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived 16 kilometers, which is about 10 miles from the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, before we get to that, you separated from your mother and sister two weeks prior to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Why did you separate from your mother and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- since, since-- as I pointed out, Katowice was about 10 miles, give and take a few, from the border. And the Poles had a big mouth at the time, said, we are going to be-- in one week, we're going to be in Berlin. He said, well, it would be safer if my mother, who was not quite well-- and she just had been-- after a bout with [INAUDIBLE]. My mother and my sister would be a little bit farther away from the border. And there was school vacation still in, in, in August.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he sent them into central Poland, into a town called Konskie, K-O-N-S-K-I-E. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had some business associates who had promised they would look after mother a little bit. And so they went two weeks before the-- two or three weeks before the war started.

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed with my father-- back. My father's boss was in England. So my father was running the company for which he worked at the time, which was a scrap and, and, and metal dealer. And I was an intern in a brick and ceramics factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the war broke out, my father went to work. And I went to work at noon on September 1st. At about 3 o'clock, we had a shell-- a German artillery shell fall on the factory, which wounded some military people who had been using our smithy to shoe their horses.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by then, my boss, who was a Jewish fraternity brother of my father's-- which is-- which is important in one way also-- said, well, that's it. And we tried to extinguish the kilns. And by then, my father came and said, let's go. We can't stay here. We tried to join my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: Obviously, you knew about Adolf Hitler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Obviously, you knew about Adolf Hitler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I had seen the Hitler Youth marching in, in, in Beuthen as a kid. And I-- when I looked out-- I told you we lived on a main street-- and I saw them, the troops, marching with, with, with candle-- no, not candle, what do you call them-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] flares at night, and, and with drum-- and pipe and drum corps through the cities with their flags. I had been at one point accosted by-- when I came back from a [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] choir practice, I had my, my prayer book, my siddur under my arm. I was accosted by two kids who beat me up and threw my prayer book that it fell down. And it fell down so that it was open, and two pages got dirty. And every time since when we came into a temple to that point, I was very embarrassed about that, that, that part. We knew that-- I had known anti-Semitism as a kid, found out about it. I had seen the Nazis come to power. I was in 1930 in the Baltic Sea. And I saw them getting on the-- on the beaches, around with their-- with their Hakenkreuz, with their swastikas. And so I was aware. Nobody was aware how it was going to develop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what were you thinking when the war started on September 1, 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's get out of here. That was one thing I felt because that was the first time I heard, really, shooting. And I was-- heard the airplanes flying around. I didn't-- on the first day, except for the artillery shell, I did not see any ordnance landing anywhere. But I heard it going all day long, from the morning when I got up. And I was frightened when I saw the people running away. There was a stream of people with hand wagons-- hand-carried wagons, and with horses, and without anything, carrying stuff on their back, and just going and going. Some probably had a idea where they were going. Maybe they had relatives a little bit farther away. But nobody knew-- nobody knew that in the city of Katowice, where I was born, the Germans would be three or four days after the war had started. At that point, if you didn't run fast, it didn't matter. I mean, we were lucky at that point when we got out as far as we did get out. But people were trying to run away with cars. And they would run out of gasoline and would leave their cars standing on the streets, on the roads, on highways. Because there was nowhere to go anymore. People would leave their belongings in their cars. I know of friends and family who-- where the man, the, the father was a dentist, and one of the daughters was a dentist. The father was abroad, and-- at that time in France, and ended in Portugal, and subsequently in Montreal, Quebec. But the other daughter was with her mother fleeing. And and, and they, they left some of the gold in the car, which they had to abandon. Dentists always used to have gold. And they took some gold with them. And they left some of the gold in the car.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do I find out? Because their son came one evening to us where we were. And they had some gold left. And he asked my father, who was working for some shoe factory, if he knew a cobbler who would put gold in their shoes so that when they get out from there, they could take it with them. Your-- so the war begins. And you and your father decide that you're going to go-- try to get your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you leave immediately on the September 1st?

speaker: interviewee
text: We leave September 1. We had-- we lived in a suburb called Piotrowice, and-- which was about three stops on the commuter train. It was a city. And we had passes. So we-- there we didn't need a ticket. We had the monthly pass. And we came into Katowice, in the main railroad station. And there is a sign posted over each of the windows that if you want a ticket for more than 20 kilometers, you have to have a permit from the county administrator. And well, that was nonsense.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we bought a ticket. We looked at-- there was a-- there was a board, which was light-- where always the trains would light up, which were going out. And there was a train, it lighted-- the light went on. It said there's a train going to Myslowice and Oswiemcim. So we went, and we bought a ticket to Oswiemcim. Well, needless to say, we didn't know-- by then, Oswiemcim was a town that was about probably 50% Jewish population. I don't know. I didn't count them, but approximately. The town is better known as something else. What is it--

speaker: interviewee
text: But that--

speaker: interviewer
text: --better known as?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that town, town, town is-- that town is better known as Auschwitz. But interestingly enough, it was-- contrary to what people think-- it was Auschwitz long before it became Oswiemcim. I just had some research on it. It was Auschwitz already in the Twelfth Century.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] And we got to Auschwitz. And there was another train there. And we said, where is it going? Because you just took wherever a train was going away east, away from the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't mean to interrupt you. But did you and your father go home and pack anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so you were just--

speaker: interviewee
text: --we packed. We packed something. We packed something, and we packed the wrong things. We packed absolute-- I took my stamp collection. And I took some books with formulas for ceramic glazes, and-- which I had worked out in the lab and stuff. And we took some clothes. But we didn't take any of the winter clothes because it was September. And anybody who lived at the time-- September, 1939, to the detriment of the Poles, was a beautiful weather. If it would have rained, the Germans might have gotten stuck in the mud somewhere because Polish roads were not very good. But it was brilliant weather. It was warm. So we had the wrong stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. I didn't mean to interrupt you. I'm sorry. So the-- so you, you decide to take another train at that point.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we took another train. And the train went to-- they said the train's going to go to Cracow. Now, what we had with us was a map of Polish railroad lights. So we didn't have a schedule because a schedule was meaningless anyhow. But that had been part of a schedule. And we took that with us so that we could follow it, perhaps make some plans. The plans were good for nothing. We went to-- we didn't even go to the main station in Cracow. We went to one of the-- around the city. And we stopped shortly on the station on the place which is famous in Plaszow. And that's-- so I-- we have two places already. We have Oswiemcim, Auschwitz, and we have Plaszow. It's not the rest-- it's not all. We come to others which have become notorious. And the train-- but by that time, we still are on passenger trains. And we had only one ticket to Auschwitz. And since then, we hadn't bought any tickets. And nobody bothered to ask us. We were hungry. We hadn't any food with us. So the train wasn't really going very fast. We-- sometimes we were standing for two hours under a semaphore, under a signal. And then some farmers would come and sell something to eat. In one place, we got some rutabagas, I think, from the field. And we're eating raw rutabagas or so. It didn't matter-- whatever there was. Then, we came to a place called Debica, D-E with a little tail B-I-C-A. There, we got off the train because not far from there was a Jewish farm in which I had spent vacation once. And I said, that would be a good place to be because probably the farm they wouldn't bomb. And it would be nice to go there. But first, we went into the city. And as we walked out of the station, there were two big craters, huge, from bombs. And the one had been a hotel once. And there was nothing left. And we went in the city. And I see, like today, there is a dairy store. And there's a sign in the window, 'We're back soon.' And inside, there is such a massive block of butter with a knife in it. And there's nobody there. But the door is closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father went-- and we went in the center. And my father got a shave there at the barber shop. And there was a sausage factory, which had a little store open. And they were selling sausage by the yard. There was a big basket and Polish kielbasa sausage. There was a guy who was selling it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we bought, I don't know, a yard or two yards of the sausage. And we bought some bread because we had been hungry terribly-- in a bakery. And we had a whole loaf of bread, and we had some sausage. And we went back to the railroad stations. It turned out there was no train going to that place where I-- where the farm was. So we hopped on the next train which came by, which was a freight train. And we went somewhere. And really, instead of getting closer to my mother, we were getting farther away. But then, we came to a station where there was again a railroad junction. And there was a train going in that direction where mother was supposed to be. This time it was nice passenger cars, so comfortable. And there was one guy who was running-- we watched. We were moving and standing, moving. And every time, a plane was showing up-- a German plane was doing this strange stop, then everybody hopped out into the ditches. And there was a guy there with a-- some medal on his civilian suit. And he said, I had to be two hours ago, with the general staff in Warsaw. And he never got there. Well, we stopped in one city for about two days, probably, day and a half. My father went to the market there, which was, in a way, important-- Rudnik on the River San-- because later on, a physician from there was our caretaker in a way. And then the train went for a few miles farther. And then the machinist said, I live in that village. And the next bridge, about a few miles away has been destroyed. So the train isn't going any farther. So you do what you want, I'm going home.

speaker: interviewer
text: He just left the train sitting there in the middle of the tracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: He left-- he extinguished-- there's a fire in there because it was steam locomotives-- extinguished the fire in the locomotive, let the steam out, and went home. And we went in the fields. And we were hiding from the German planes that were flying over there. And came in up to a, a little town called Stalowa Wola-- which means steel will, 'will' from-- not well, but from 'to want something'-- which was an industrial center the Poles built to have something-- just be farther away from the border. And there, some people offered us to buy some horses to go away. But we declined. And then some other people offered us to take us in a car with them. It was employees from an oil company who were going to their headquarters. There were already three people in the car. And it was full of luggage. And we paid a princely sum to be taken to the city of Lublin [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and never got there. We saw-- that's when I really saw, for the first time, war damages. We came through a little town, Janow Lubelski, J-A-N-O with a dash, W, and then dash, Lubelski, L-U-B-E-L-S-K-I. And there, a lumberyard was burning. There was-- had been a, a straight hit in the lumberyard. And there were dead horses on the street, all over the place. And the place looked just terrible. And we drove through a sand desert. We came to a town where we were able-- Modliborzyce, where we were able to buy some bread and slept overnight in the car. Because as we were on the way to Lublin, we were told they don't let any people into Lublin. Lublin is being bombed by the Germans. It doesn't make any sense to go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we drove us to a town called Bilgoraj, B-I-L-G-O-R-A-J-- notorious. All the Jews from there were killed later on. It was a predominantly Jewish town. Somebody took us in for a few hours to wash up a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we took a nightmarish train ride on a narrow-gage railroad on platform cars, which were shaking like the devil. And there was nothing to hold on to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we ended up in another notorious-- later place, called Belzec. That's where we met the main railroad. There, as we crossed the street, a car passed by and stopped, and a guy came out and was calling my father's name. It was at Jew who was an officer in the Polish intelligence. He had been in the Polish intelligence in the war against the Soviets. And those veterans had gotten later on, regardless if they were Jews or not, some privileges. And he had gotten a license to buy scrap iron from military sources and from government places and sell them. So that's how my father knew him, from the scrap-iron business. But when the war started, he had been drafted again. He was English intelligence. And he said to my father, Dr. Guttmann, Dr. Guttmann, I-- don't go anywhere. Stay in the railroad station. I will be back in half an hour. And I take you with me in my car.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I don't know what his real name was, but his name was Polanski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Okay, thank you. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we sat down in the station. And about half an hour, the stationmaster comes out and says, is a Dr. Guttmann here? Yeah. Well, we just had a telephone call from the gentleman who talked to you on the road. His orders have changed. He is not coming back. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we hopped on the next train which came, which was so full. There were those European-style, express-train cars, with a corridor and compartments inside. Well, we got pressed into the toilet at the end of the corridor and rode with the window open, three or four people standing [LAUGHS] in that toilet until we came to the city of Lemberg in Austria-- it was called. In Polish, it was Lwow, L-W-O-W. L-W-O-W. And now in Ukrainians call it Lwiw, L-W-I-W.

speaker: interviewee
text: We stopped at the outskirts of the city. And the first thing that we saw, there was a flour mill-- [NON-ENGLISH] I think it was called-- in flames. It was burned. Went into the city, and there were constantly air alarms, air alerts, in the house, and, and hide. And my father went to visit a rabbi, who, at one time, had been a rabbi in our city. He was very non-committal and would not say much to my father and neither help nor nothing. And so we went-- first of all, being hungry, in the-- found the Jewish restaurant and had something to eat there. And then we found out that, from another station at night, there would be a train going to the-- towards the Romanian border. So we walked-- we took a streetcar into that direction in the evening. And then we walked the rest to the station. And at that point, we met [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] four or five other men whom we knew. Two were-- two or three were from our city. The one was the father of-- the uncle-- or the brother-in-law-- I'm sorry-- the brother-in-law, Mr. Taller-- the brother-in-law of a former school friend of mine. But she had lived-- she was-- had lived with them because her mother and father had died early. So she lived with her older sister, with this brother-in-law. And it turned out, I found out later, the other one was the fellow who was, by then already, unbeknownst to me, the husband of that girl who had been in my class. She had gotten married early. And one was a textile manufacturer from Bielsko-- I think I had mentioned before. So now we were seven of us together.
